content.text: Examples are the most reliable way to communicate format and style requirements to a language model. An instruction like "write in a casual tone" leaves the model guessing what casual means to you; two examples of casual tone show it exactly. This technique — providing input-output pairs in the prompt — is called few-shot prompting when applied to a whole task, but the same principle applies narrowly to format: even a single well-chosen example eliminates most format ambiguity. Examples are particularly powerful when the desired output has a structure that is hard to describe in words but easy to demonstrate.
When Examples Outperform Instructions
Situation
Instructions alone
Examples
Custom output format
Hard to specify precisely
One example fully conveys the structure
Stylistic tone
"Casual" is ambiguous
Two example sentences anchor the register exactly
Edge-case handling
"Handle missing values" — how?
Show the desired output for a missing-value input
Classification labels with nuance
Label definitions overlap and are hard to specify
Borderline examples show where the boundary is
Standard task (translation, summary)
Sufficient — model knows the task well
Examples add tokens but little extra signal
Format-Anchoring with a Single Example

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def ask(prompt: str) -> str:
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=150,
        temperature=0.0,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


# Without example: model guesses what "concise changelog entry" means
print(ask(
    "Write a concise changelog entry for: added dark mode toggle to settings page"
))
print()

# With example: format is unambiguous
print(ask("""
Write a concise changelog entry following the format below.

Example:
Input: fixed crash when uploading files over 100MB
Output: **Fix** — Resolved crash during upload of files exceeding 100 MB.

Now write one for:
Input: added dark mode toggle to settings page
Output:"""))
# → **Feature** — Added dark mode toggle to the Settings page.
Few-Shot Classification with Multiple Examples

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


def few_shot_classify(
    examples: list[tuple[str, str]],
    query: str,
    labels: list[str],
) -> str:
    """
    Build a few-shot classification prompt from (input, label) pairs.
    The pattern of examples teaches the model the label boundaries.
    """
    shots = "\n".join(
        f"Text: {text}\nLabel: {label}" for text, label in examples
    )
    prompt = (
        f"Classify the text. Valid labels: {', '.join(labels)}.\n\n"
        f"{shots}\n\n"
        f"Text: {query}\nLabel:"
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=10,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


EXAMPLES = [
    ("The checkout process is smooth and intuitive.",           "Positive"),
    ("I've been waiting 3 weeks for a refund.",                "Negative"),
    ("Delivery was on time.",                                  "Neutral"),
    ("Absolutely love the new design — so clean!",             "Positive"),
    ("App crashes every time I open the camera.",              "Negative"),
]

TEST_CASES = [
    "The product is fine, does what it says.",
    "Customer support resolved my issue within an hour.",
    "Terrible experience. Would not recommend.",
]

for text in TEST_CASES:
    label = few_shot_classify(EXAMPLES, text, ["Positive", "Negative", "Neutral"])
    print(f"{label:10s}  {text}")
Choosing and Ordering Examples
Cover the label distribution.
If your task has three classes, include at least one example of each. A skewed set of examples biases the model toward the over-represented class.
Include borderline cases.
The most useful examples are the ones near the decision boundary — they teach the model where one label ends and another begins.
Keep format consistent.
Use the same delimiter pattern ("Text:", "Label:") in every example and in the query. Inconsistency causes the model to follow the format of the last example it saw.
Put the query last.
Models give more weight to recently seen tokens. The query should always be the last item, immediately before where the model generates.
3–5 examples is usually the sweet spot.
More examples improve robustness but consume context tokens. Beyond 8–10 examples, returns diminish; consider fine-tuning instead.
What It Is

Using examples in prompts — commonly called few-shot prompting — is the technique of including one or more input-output pairs in the context window to demonstrate, rather than describe, what the model should do. The term 'few-shot' comes from the machine learning literature, where it describes learning a task from a small number of labelled examples. In the context of prompting, the analogy is that the model reads your examples and adjusts its generation to follow the demonstrated pattern, without any weight update.

This is distinct from fine-tuning: fine-tuning modifies the model's weights using gradient descent on a training set; few-shot prompting provides examples only in the context window. The effect on the model's output can be dramatic — especially for format and style — but it is local to that call and consumed each time, paying a token cost on every request.

The spectrum runs from zero-shot (no examples, rely on instruction and pre-training) through one-shot (a single demonstration) to few-shot (typically 3–8 examples). The right number depends on how complex and idiosyncratic the task is. Standard tasks that the model has seen extensively in training (translation, summarisation, sentiment classification with common labels) often require zero or one examples. Custom tasks — novel output formats, niche domain labels, proprietary style conventions — typically benefit from 3–5 carefully chosen examples.
Why It Matters

The core pain point examples solve is the gap between what you can describe in words and what the model actually produces. Natural language instructions are inherently ambiguous: 'write a brief summary' could mean a single sentence or three paragraphs; 'use a professional tone' could mean formal academic or approachable business. When you show the model exactly what you want, that ambiguity collapses. A single output example communicates format, length, vocabulary level, capitalization conventions, and stylistic register simultaneously — things that would take many paragraphs of instructions to approximate.

Examples are especially powerful for classification tasks with nuanced boundaries. Consider labelling customer support tickets as 'billing', 'technical', or 'general'. The words alone don't determine the label — 'my app keeps crashing' is technical, but 'my app keeps crashing and you charged me twice' is both. A list of label definitions won't capture every edge case. A handful of examples near the decision boundary does.

From an engineering perspective, examples also provide stability. When you update a model or change prompt wording, examples act as an anchor — the model is less likely to drift in format if it has concrete demonstrations to follow. This makes prompts with examples more robust to minor model version changes than purely instructional prompts.
Benefits
Eliminates format ambiguity.
A single output example communicates structure, length, vocabulary, and punctuation conventions that would require paragraphs to specify in instructions.
Works without fine-tuning.
Achieving consistent format compliance with examples requires no training data collection, no GPU, and no deployment cycle. It is a prompt change.
Handles edge cases explicitly.
You can show the model exactly how to handle a tricky input type — missing values, ambiguous phrasing, dual-category items — by including that specific case as an example.
Improves classification label adherence.
Few-shot examples significantly reduce label hallucination — the model generating a label you didn't define. Seeing your exact label set in examples informs the model of the allowed output space.
Transferable across similar tasks.
A well-designed example set for one subtask can often be reused or lightly adapted for similar subtasks, amortising the curation cost.
Trade-offs
Token cost.
Each example consumes context tokens. Five examples of 100 tokens each add 500 tokens to every request — that is 500 tokens of input that don't carry information about the current query.
Curation effort.
Poorly chosen examples can bias the model. An example set that over-represents one class, or uses an atypical style, will steer output in the wrong direction. Good examples require thought.
Recency and ordering effects.

Models attend more to examples near the end of the context. The last example before the query has outsized influence on output style. This can be exploited but also introduces variance if example order is shuffled.
Not a substitute for fine-tuning at scale.

Few-shot examples work well up to 8–10 shots. Beyond that, returns diminish and you are spending more tokens than a fine-tuned model would cost per request. For high-volume, consistent-format tasks, fine-tuning on labelled data outperforms in-context learning.
Example staleness.
If your examples become unrepresentative of production input (distribution shift), the model's example-conditioned output degrades. Examples need periodic review.
How It Works — In-Context Learning Mechanics

Language models process the entire context — instructions, examples, and query — as a single token sequence. When the model generates the next token, every earlier token in the context influences it through attention. Examples function as a strong inductive signal: the model observes a pattern
input_1 → output_1, input_2 → output_2
, and when presented with
input_3 →
, it continues the pattern.
This is called
in-context learning (ICL)

— the model adapts its behavior based on context without weight updates. ICL works because large language models are trained on diverse text that includes many implicit input-output sequences, giving them a meta-learning ability: they learn how to learn from examples.

The effectiveness of ICL scales with model size. Larger models show stronger few-shot performance — they can infer the pattern from fewer, noisier examples. Smaller models may require more examples, more consistent formatting, and simpler patterns to reliably follow.
Architecture — Few-Shot Prompt Flow

attrs.alt: Few-shot prompting architecture: prompt structure with task instruction, examples, and query flowing into the LLM to produce format-consistent output

content.text: How to Implement — Step by Step
Define the task and output format.
Write one ideal output for a representative input before writing any code. This is your first example and your quality bar.
Choose 3–5 examples that cover the distribution.
Include at least one typical case, one edge case, and (for classification) at least one example per class.
Establish a consistent delimiter pattern.
Choose a pattern like
Input: ... Output: ...
and use it identically in every example and in the query.
Order examples with the query last.
The model attends more to recent context. Put the query immediately before the generation point, and put your most representative example immediately before the query.
Use stop sequences to cleanly truncate output.
If your output ends at a newline, set
stop_sequences=['\n']
to prevent the model from generating additional examples.
Evaluate and iterate.
Run the prompt on a held-out set of inputs and measure accuracy / format compliance. Replace examples that are misleading or that don't improve coverage.

# Step-by-step few-shot implementation with evaluation
import anthropic
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

client = anthropic.Anthropic()


@dataclass
class Example:
    input: str
    output: str


def build_few_shot_prompt(
    task_instruction: str,
    examples: list[Example],
    query_input: str,
    input_label: str = "Input",
    output_label: str = "Output",
) -> str:
    """
    Build a few-shot prompt with a consistent delimiter pattern.
    The query is always last, immediately before the generation point.
    """
    lines = [task_instruction, ""]
    for ex in examples:
        lines.append(f"{input_label}: {ex.input}")
        lines.append(f"{output_label}: {ex.output}")
        lines.append("")  # blank line between examples
    lines.append(f"{input_label}: {query_input}")
    lines.append(f"{output_label}:")  # leave blank — model completes this
    return "\n".join(lines)


def run_few_shot(
    task_instruction: str,
    examples: list[Example],
    query: str,
    max_tokens: int = 50,
    stop_at_newline: bool = True,
) -> str:
    prompt = build_few_shot_prompt(task_instruction, examples, query)
    stop_seqs = ["\n"] if stop_at_newline else []
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=max_tokens,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=stop_seqs,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return response.content[0].text.strip()


# --- Example: bug severity classification ---
TASK = "Classify the severity of the bug report. Valid labels: critical, high, medium, low."

EXAMPLES = [
    Example("App crashes on login for all users — no one can access the service.", "critical"),
    Example("Export to CSV fails for files over 10MB.", "high"),
    Example("Tooltip text is slightly misaligned on mobile.", "low"),
    Example("Search results take 4 seconds to load on slow connections.", "medium"),
]

TEST_CASES = [
    "Payment processing is down — no transactions going through.",    # expected: critical
    "The 'forgot password' link doesn't work on Safari.",              # expected: high
    "The welcome email has a typo in the footer.",                     # expected: low
]

for case in TEST_CASES:
    result = run_few_shot(TASK, EXAMPLES, case)
    print(f"{result:10s}  {case[:60]}")
# critical    Payment processing is down — no transactions going thr...
# high        The 'forgot password' link doesn't work on Safari.
# low         The welcome email has a typo in the footer.
Real-World Example — Structured Changelog Generation

A development team uses an LLM to auto-generate changelog entries from git commit messages. The entries must follow a strict house style: action word in bold, consistent tense, emoji category prefix, and a maximum of one sentence. Rather than trying to describe this style in instructions (which inevitably misses edge cases), the team maintains a library of 6 example pairs that capture every category and tone variation.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

CHANGELOG_EXAMPLES = [
    ("fix: null pointer when user has no profile image",
     "**Fix** — Resolved crash caused by missing profile image during account load."),
    ("feat: add dark mode toggle to settings",
     "**Feature** — Added dark mode toggle in Settings > Appearance."),
    ("perf: lazy-load dashboard widgets",
     "**Performance** — Dashboard widgets now load on-demand, reducing initial page load by 40%."),
    ("chore: bump lodash to 4.17.21",
     "**Security** — Updated lodash to 4.17.21 to address CVE-2021-23337."),
    ("refactor: extract email validation into shared util",
     "**Refactor** — Centralised email validation logic into a reusable utility module."),
    ("docs: update API auth section with token expiry notes",
     "**Docs** — Clarified token expiry behaviour in the API Authentication reference."),
]


def generate_changelog_entry(commit_msg: str) -> str:
    shots = "\n\n".join(
        f"Commit: {inp}\nEntry: {out}" for inp, out in CHANGELOG_EXAMPLES
    )
    prompt = (
        "Convert the git commit message to a changelog entry. "
        "Follow the format exactly as shown in the examples.\n\n"
        f"{shots}\n\n"
        f"Commit: {commit_msg}\nEntry:"
    )
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=80,
        temperature=0.0,
        stop_sequences=["\n"],
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    return resp.content[0].text.strip()


COMMITS = [
    "fix: calendar event disappears after timezone change",
    "feat: export reports as PDF",
    "perf: cache user preferences in Redis",
]

for commit in COMMITS:
    entry = generate_changelog_entry(commit)
    print(f"  {entry}")
# **Fix** — Resolved calendar event loss when user changes timezone.
# **Feature** — Added PDF export for reports.
# **Performance** — User preferences now cached in Redis to reduce database load.
Common Mistakes
Imbalanced class coverage.
Providing 4 'positive' examples and 1 'negative' example for binary classification biases the model toward positive labels. Always balance representation across classes.
Inconsistent formatting between examples.
If some examples use 'Input:' and others use 'TEXT:', the model treats them as different patterns. Use the same field names, capitalisation, and punctuation in every example.
Putting the query in the middle.
The query should always be last, directly before the generation point. A query embedded between examples gets treated as another example, not as the task.
Using examples for tasks where instructions suffice.

For well-known tasks like 'translate to French' or 'summarise this article', examples add tokens without meaningfully improving accuracy. Reserve examples for tasks with non-obvious format or edge-case-heavy classification.
Not using stop sequences.
Without a stop sequence, the model may generate additional fake examples after the query output. Setting
stop_sequences=['\n\n']
cleanly terminates generation after the output.
Best Practices
Start with one example and measure.
A single well-chosen example often closes most of the format gap. Add more examples only if evaluation shows specific failure modes that the additional example would address.
Use real data, not synthetic examples.
Craft examples from actual inputs and their ideal outputs. Synthetic examples that 'look like' the task but don't come from real distribution often miss the edge cases that matter.
Include a 'hard' example near the end.
Put your most informative (borderline or unusual) example second-to-last in the prompt — it will have high influence on the query output due to recency.
Version-control your example sets.
Examples are part of your product logic. Track changes, review them in PRs, and test them against a held-out evaluation set when modified.
Know when to switch to fine-tuning.
When you have 500+ high-quality labelled examples and the task runs at volume, fine-tuning reduces per-request token cost and often improves accuracy compared to in-context learning.
Performance and Scalability
The token cost of examples scales linearly with the number of examples and their length. At
$3/M input tokens
(a typical mid-range model pricing), a 5-shot prompt adding 500 tokens adds
per request. At 10,000 requests per day that is $15/day in additional cost — real but often justified by the reliability improvement over zero-shot.
Use prompt caching.

Claude and OpenAI both offer prompt caching that amortises the cost of repeated examples. Cache the system + examples section; only the query varies per request. This typically reduces effective input cost by 70–90% on cacheable prefixes.
Token budget.

Keep examples short. An example with a 30-word input and a 10-word output costs ~60 tokens. Five such examples cost 300 tokens. A verbose 100-word-per-example set costs 500+ tokens — at this point, fine-tuning economics become attractive.
Latency.
More input tokens means slightly higher time-to-first-token latency (the model must attend over a longer context). For latency-sensitive applications, minimise example count and length.
Tooling and Ecosystem
LangChain FewShotPromptTemplate.

A template class that handles example insertion, formatting, and (optionally) dynamic example selection based on similarity to the query. Useful for large example libraries where you select the k most relevant examples per query.
DSPy.
A framework for programmatic prompt optimisation that can automatically select and arrange examples to maximise a metric. Useful for automating the example curation process at scale.
Anthropic prompt caching.
Cache the prefix containing examples using the
cache_control
API parameter. Reduces repeated example token cost by ~90% for high-volume applications.
Key Takeaways
Examples communicate format, style, and edge-case handling more efficiently than instructions alone — a single example can replace many paragraphs of specification.
In-context learning requires no weight updates. The model adapts to your examples within a single call, making few-shot prompting a zero-infrastructure technique.
3–5 examples is typically the sweet spot. Cover the label distribution, include borderline cases, and use consistent delimiter patterns throughout.
Put the query last. Models give more weight to recent context; the query should be immediately before the generation point to benefit from recency.
Use stop sequences to prevent the model from generating additional examples after the query output.
At high volume, use prompt caching (Anthropic, OpenAI) to amortise the token cost of the examples prefix. Consider fine-tuning when you have 500+ examples and volume justifies it.
Examples don't substitute for evaluation. Measure format compliance and task accuracy on a held-out set after adding or changing examples.

An example is worth a hundred words of specification. When you cannot easily describe a rule but you can recognise the right answer when you see it, demonstrating it in the prompt is both faster and more reliable than trying to articulate it. The constraint is token budget: each example costs context space, so choose examples that carry the most information — specifically, those that cover boundaries and edge cases your instruction text cannot capture.
